title: UK Defence Plan Released After 9-Month Delay; Hormuz Traffic Remains Disrupted; Australia-Vanuatu Pact Blocks Chinese Bases
subhead: Britain's long-delayed Defence Investment Plan is set for release Tuesday after a nine-month gap that collapsed small suppliers. The Strait of Hormuz remains below normal traffic after fresh attacks on vessels. Australia and Vanuatu signed a security pact barring foreign military bases, directly countering China's Pacific influence.

sections.heading: UK Defence Investment Plan Emerges After Nine-Month Delay That Hollowed Out Suppliers
sections.body:
Britain's Defence Investment Plan — the spending blueprint meant to guide procurement against a rising Russian threat — is expected to be published on Tuesday, concluding a nine-month delay that Prime Minister Keir Starmer's office attributes to internal disputes between the defence and finance ministries over how to bridge an anticipated £28 billion gap.

The delay has already done real damage. According to Reuters and Devdiscourse reporting, some small suppliers have collapsed, others have held off investments, and many UK defence firms have pivoted to international business as domestic contract awards from the Ministry of Defence dried up. Executives including Shefali Sharma and Andrew Thomis of Cohort and Q5D have highlighted missed local opportunities, with companies now considering foreign production sites. The MOD claims to have signed numerous contracts since July last year, but industry says the pipeline has been insufficient and unpredictable.

Bloomberg reports the plan allocates around £5 billion to drones and AI, signaling a pivot toward unmanned and autonomous systems — a direction consistent with how both Ukraine and Russia are reshaping the battlefield. The question is whether the spending plan arrives fast enough to repair a supply chain that has already been losing capacity to competitors abroad.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK is the second-largest defence spender in NATO. A nine-month procurement vacuum has already forced capacity out of the domestic supply chain — capacity that cannot be switched back on with a press release.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz: Attacks on Two Vessels Slow Traffic Recovery, Iran-Oman Open Joint Committee
sections.body:
Commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains below normal after two separate attacks on vessels renewed security concerns. A Singapore-registered container ship, Ever Lovely, sustained minor damage to its bridge area from an unknown projectile on June 25 while exiting the strait, according to Singapore's Maritime and Port Authority. Two days later, US Central Command reported that Iranian forces struck the Panama-flagged tanker M/T Kiku with a one-way attack drone while it carried more than 2 million barrels of crude oil near the strait.

Tracking data from Windward showed 40 total transits on June 27 (24 inbound, 16 outbound), with inbound traffic tanker-heavy and featuring strong Iranian-flagged vessel representation. Several VLCCs continued entering the Gulf to load crude, but the Joint Maritime Information Center raised the security threat level to 'substantial,' warning vessels about mines and naval activity linked to mine-clearing operations.

The route itself has become a flashpoint: Tehran insists vessels use Iranian-authorised routes, while Washington backs safe commercial passage through the strait. Meanwhile, Iran and Oman held the first meeting of a joint committee on the strait in Muscat, with Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi describing discussions within the framework of the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding and 'the sovereign rights of coastal states.' Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi stated that responsibility for the strait lies solely with Tehran.

Oil markets have responded: Brent crude slid toward $70/barrel, with analysts noting that 130-150 million barrels of US-authorized Iranian oil have hit the market as a one-time supply effect even as transit risks persist.
sections.whyItMatters: Hormuz is the world's most critical energy chokepoint. The fact that traffic is resuming but remains disrupted — with competing claims over which routes are legitimate — means the diplomatic framework around the strait is being actively renegotiated, not simply restored.

sections.heading: Australia-Vanuatu Nakamal Agreement Bars Foreign Military Bases, Countering China in the Pacific
sections.body:
Australia and Vanuatu signed the Nakamal Agreement on Monday, a bilateral security and economic treaty that explicitly prevents any foreign military base or militarised infrastructure on Vanuatu's territory. Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese called it a reflection of Australia's role as Vanuatu's 'largest and most comprehensive economic, security and development partner.' Vanuatu's Prime Minister Jotham Napat described it as founded on 'mutual respect, trust and our common vision for a peaceful, stable and prosperous Pacific.'

The treaty had been delayed for nine months after Vanuatu rejected an earlier draft over concerns it could limit infrastructure investment. Under the final agreement, Vanuatu will consult with Australia when considering third-party engagement in critical infrastructure, though there is no Australian veto power as originally proposed. Vanuatu also committed to prioritise policing cooperation with Pacific Islands Forum members and to turn to Australia, New Zealand, and France first for disaster response.

China's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Guo Jiakun said Beijing hoped the agreement would 'contribute to the development and stability of the island region, not target any third party.' Separately, Napat said Vanuatu's bilateral 'Namele Agreement' with China — described as a 'comprehensive development cooperation' deal, not a security pact — would be made public once it receives 'clearance from Beijing.' The original draft had proposed A$500 million over a decade; Albanese said the cost of the final deal would be disclosed by December.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the latest in a string of Australian Pacific pacts designed to close off Chinese security access. The removal of the veto clause shows Vanuatu extracted meaningful concessions — but the core prohibition on foreign bases remains, and that is the provision Beijing cares about most.

sections.heading: Russia Recruits University Students for Drone Forces as Casualties Mount
sections.body:
Russia is systematically recruiting female university students for its Unmanned Systems Forces, created in November 2025, leveraging drone warfare's demand for technical skills over physical strength. According to the Russian human rights project Get Lost, the Defense Ministry approached female students at at least 10 colleges and universities with offers of academic leave and financial incentives to serve as drone operators, portraying the role as a relatively safe technical profession.

A February Jamestown Foundation report identified drone operator curricula at 32 Russian universities, creating a long-term recruitment pipeline. Bloomberg reported in May that Russia is recruiting programmers, gamers, and drone pilots from colleges. Samuel Bendett, an adjunct senior fellow at the Center for a New American Security, told Forbes that 'there are many women in Russia who have the required skills' for unmanned systems.

The reality behind the recruitment pitch is stark. A June BBC Russian Service investigation found that several students recruited as drone operators were initially assigned to assault units despite their specific contracts. The BBC has identified more than 1,000 Russian drone operators killed during the war. Former Rubicon drone operator Miroslav Simonov has described drone specialists being threatened with reassignment to assault units if they failed to meet expectations. Serhii Kuzan of the Ukrainian Security and Cooperation Center told Forbes that Russia is 'trading personnel safety for operational effectiveness' by moving operators closer to the front line.

Ukrainian operators confirm the investment is paying off. Dmytro Zhlutenko of Ukraine's 413th Separate Battalion 'Raid' told Forbes that Russian drone effectiveness 'is continuing to grow undoubtedly,' with intensified recruiting at leading technical universities alongside elite formations like Rubicon attracting skilled volunteers.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's institutionalisation of drone warfare through a dedicated service branch and university pipeline reflects how unmanned systems have moved from tactical tool to strategic priority — but the gap between recruitment promises and actual deployment reveals the attrition-based model that still governs Russia's war.

sections.heading: South Korea Launches First Full Four-Stage Military Rocket, Joining Elite Club
sections.body:
South Korea's Agency for Defense Development fired the first complete four-stage version of its classified solid-fuel military rocket (GYŪB) from an offshore barge near Jeju Island early Monday. The vehicle carries a payload capacity of approximately 500-700 kg to low Earth orbit — a significant jump from the three-stage predecessor that placed a 100-kg SAR satellite into a 650-km orbit in December 2023.

The rocket exists for one military requirement: reducing satellite revisit time over North Korea from 2 hours to 30 minutes. South Korea's current five-satellite 425 Project constellation delivers coverage every two hours; the goal requires a supplementary constellation of 19 smaller SAR satellites launched across seven flights through 2027. Monday's demo flight is the first of those seven.

If confirmed successful, South Korea becomes the sixth nation — after the US, Russia, China, France, and Israel — to operate an indigenous solid-fuel military space launch vehicle. The programme was made possible by the 2021 termination of 42 years of US-imposed restrictions on South Korean solid-fuel rocket development, lifted at a Biden-Moon summit.

The first stage produces approximately 245 tonnes of thrust — roughly 1.5 times the output of North Korea's solid-fuel rocket engines, according to South Korea's defence ministry. This comes as Pyongyang completed a ground-jet test for a solid-fuel ICBM engine designed to carry multiple warheads earlier this spring, according to Stars and Stripes and UPI reporting.
sections.whyItMatters: Both Koreas are racing on solid-fuel missile technology, but in opposite directions — the North toward nuclear-capable ICBMs, the South toward rapid-response reconnaissance satellites. The end of US restrictions on South Korean solid-fuel rockets has opened a capability gap that Seoul is moving quickly to fill.

sections.heading: Pakistan-Afghanistan Border: Airstrikes Kill Dozens as Diplomatic Fallout Escalates
sections.body:
Pakistan carried out overnight airstrikes along the Afghanistan border targeting what it described as militant hideouts, killing at least 29 fighters according to Islamabad and 36 civilians including women and children according to the Taliban. The UN Assistance Mission in Afghanistan confirmed civilian casualties, putting preliminary figures at at least 28 civilians killed and 49 injured across Paktia, Paktika, and Kunar provinces.

Pakistan's Information Minister Attaullah Tarar said the 'calibrated strikes' followed a ground operation in Bajaur district and targeted Jamaat-ul-Ahrar and what Pakistan calls Fitna al-Khwarij (the TTP). The operation came a day after militants attacked the regional headquarters of Pakistan's paramilitary Rangers in Karachi, killing three soldiers. Jamaat-ul-Ahrar claimed responsibility for that attack; the military identified one captured assailant as an Afghan national.

An eyewitness in Mandukhel village, Qayum Khan, told CBS News that a Pakistani fighter jet bombed a civilian home and then conducted a second strike on people who had gathered to rescue the wounded. The Taliban-run Foreign Affairs Ministry summoned Pakistan's charge d'affaires in Kabul. Afghanistan's Taliban government disputes that it harbours militants who carry out attacks inside Pakistan.
sections.whyItMatters: The escalating cycle of cross-border strikes and militant attacks is pushing two nuclear-armed neighbours toward a pattern of retaliation that neither controls. Civilian casualties — confirmed by the UN — make de-escalation harder with each round.

sections.heading: B-52 Memorial at Edwards AFB; Ukraine Fortifies Belarus Border
sections.body:
A memorial service was held Monday evening at Edwards Air Force Base for the eight people killed when a B-52 Stratofortress crashed shortly after takeoff on June 15 during a routine test mission supporting a radar modernization program. The dead included active-duty officers from the 419th Flight Test Squadron, a Boeing weapon systems officer, and a Boeing pilot. Col. James Hayes, deputy commander of the 412th Test Wing, said footage confirmed the crash was 'unrecoverable and unsurvivable.' The investigation into the cause is ongoing. It was the first fatal crash at Edwards since 1992.

Separately, Ukraine's State Border Guard Service is building a layered defense line along the Chernihiv border with Belarus, according to an on-site Ukrinform interview with border post commander Yevhen, callsign 'Nissan.' Construction volumes have increased by roughly 500% since de-occupation in 2022, using dragon's teeth anti-tank barriers (1.5 tons each, ~10m wide), reinforced Egoza razor wire, anti-tank ditches, and half-sawn tree blockages in forest areas. An excavator cuts roughly 200m of anti-tank ditch per day; two soldiers lay about 100m of razor wire in four hours. Nissan said there are currently no signs of major troop build-ups on the Belarusian side, but noted that Moscow is pressuring Minsk for permission to use Belarusian territory for drone strikes.
sections.whyItMatters: The B-52 crash during a radar modernization test underscores the risk of maintaining a bomber fleet that first entered service in 1955. Ukraine's Belarus border fortifications — a 500% scale-up since 2022 — reflect a calculation that the northern border will remain a threat regardless of any ceasefire in the east.

bottomLine: Britain's delayed defence spending plan finally lands Tuesday, but nine months of procurement vacuum have already cost the UK domestic supplier capacity it may not recover. In the Strait of Hormuz, the diplomatic architecture around the world's most important energy chokepoint is being actively reshaped — Iran and Oman have opened a joint management committee, while fresh attacks on shipping keep traffic below normal. Australia secured a Pacific pact that explicitly bans Chinese military bases in Vanuatu, though without the veto power Canberra originally wanted. Russia's institutionalisation of drone warfare now includes a university recruitment pipeline — even as BBC-confirmed data shows over 1,000 drone operators killed and recruits shunted into assault units. South Korea launched its first full four-stage solid-fuel military rocket, a capability denied to it for 42 years, aiming to cut satellite revisit time over North Korea from two hours to 30 minutes.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, Devdiscourse, AP News, Forbes, BBC Russian Service, Jamestown Foundation, Ukrinform, Kyiv Post, CBS News, SCMP, TRT World/Anadolu Agency, UPI, Tech Times, MyNewsLA, Windward, Singapore MPA, US CENTCOM, Stars and Stripes
